note: All traits derived from measured segment statistics (cluster_segments.py + segment_deep_dive.py). Weights = share of users / share of revenue. creative_lifts = % over/under-response of the segment's conversions to copy attributes vs baseline.

personas.name: Rajesh, 42, Indore
personas.profile: Small business owner entangled in a court case over a property dispute. Believes rivals and jealous relatives are blocking his progress; suspects buri nazar. Facebook feed + some reels, browses late evening. Comfortable in Hinglish, reads English.
personas.price_band: Rs.800-1500 puja, will pay for named sankalp
personas.behavior: Reads long copy fully when the problem matches his. Wants specifics: which deity, which temple, what ritual, video proof. Fear of enemies/obstacles resonates; discounts feel cheap for sacred work.

personas.name: Meena, 38, Thane
personas.profile: Homemaker; husband's career stalled, in-law friction, feels the family is 'stuck'. Instagram reels + feed. Responds to Maa Baglamukhi imagery and stories of families freed from obstacles.
personas.behavior: Stops scrolling on emotional hooks about family suffering or hidden negativity. Checklists of 'ideal for those who...' pull her in when one line names her situation.

personas.name: Suresh, 51, Delhi NCR
personas.profile: Repeat buyer, LTV over Rs.3000, 2-3 pujas across deities (Baglamukhi, then Rahu, then Kaal Bhairav). Treats remote pujas as ongoing spiritual maintenance. Cross-buys when a new ritual promises to cover a gap the last one did not.
personas.behavior: Compares this ritual to ones he has done. Rarity claims ('once in 12 years', shaktipeeth exclusivity) and completeness (havan + jaap + prasad shipped) drive him. Ignores discounts.

personas.name: Priya, 33, Bengaluru
personas.profile: IT professional, first-generation urban, spiritual but not ritual-literate. Found DevPunya via an English Facebook/Instagram FEED ad (never converts from reels). One-and-done buyer unless re-convinced from scratch.
personas.behavior: Skips Hindi-script copy entirely. Deadline urgency works ('last 20 sankalps', 'closes tonight') because she postpones otherwise. Wants legitimacy signals: named temple, Vedic priests, HD video, certificate. Crowd numbers feel spammy to her.

personas.name: Anand, 45, Hyderabad
personas.profile: NRI-adjacent manager researching remedies for shani mahadasha he read about online. English only. Compares 2-3 providers before paying; opened the ad from feed on desktop at work.
personas.behavior: Analytical skeptic: what exactly is performed, by whom, where, what do I receive. Overclaiming ('100% guaranteed results') triggers rejection. Structured copy with deliverables list converts him.

personas.name: Sunita, 47, Kanpur
personas.profile: Devout homemaker, daily WhatsApp forwards, modest budget. Buys Rs.100-200 chadhawa offerings at famous temples she cannot visit. Facebook feed + reels, Hindi-comfortable but converts on simple English too.
personas.behavior: Price on the ad is the trigger — 'Rs.101 chadhawa at Kashi Vishwanath' feels accessible and legitimate. '12,000 devotees offered this month' reassures her it is real. Big puja prices make her scroll past.

personas.name: Deepak, 29, Patna
personas.profile: Young private-job employee, orders chadhawa before exams, interviews, court dates of relatives. Instagram reels heavy. Impulse buyer: sees reel, orders in 2 minutes, forgets.
personas.behavior: Hook must land in the first line; he never expands captions. Specific low price + specific famous temple + today's tithi = conversion. Anything requiring reading loses him.

personas.name: Kamla, 58, Varanasi
personas.profile: Retired teacher, 4+ orders, treats monthly chadhawa like a standing ritual (purnima, amavasya, sankashti). The most loyal low-ticket buyer. Hindi devotional copy is her language of trust.
personas.behavior: Tithi-based hooks ('is amavasya par...') match her calendar. Salesy pressure feels disrespectful; devotional tone ('maa ke charno mein arpan karein') feels right. Recognizes and prefers rituals she has done before.

personas.name: Pooja, 26, Mumbai
personas.profile: Salon worker, deeply devotional, follows bhakti pages. Instagram reels 50%+. Converts on Hindi-script devotional copy; every Western persuasion tactic reads as fake to her.
personas.behavior: Short Hindi copy with maa/mandir emotional pull. 'Book Now' pressure, countdowns, testimonials all reduce trust. The video's devotional feel matters more than any text.

personas.name: Ramesh, 36, Lucknow
personas.profile: Shop owner, watches devotional reels at night. Occasionally upgrades from chadhawa to a small puja when the deity matches a current worry. Hindi only.
personas.behavior: Trusts what sounds like a pandit speaking, not a marketer. Simple devotional Hindi with the temple's name converts; heavy English or emoji-wall copy scrolls past.

personas.name: Vikram, 39, Pune
personas.profile: Real-estate broker with stuck deals and a land dispute. Recently acquired via Varahi campaigns (this segment is surging in Q3 2026). Facebook feed, watches full videos but skims text.
personas.behavior: Hooks naming HIS world work: property, land, builders, big businessmen, court dates. Both fear (deal blocked by hidden forces) and aspiration (unlock wealth) convert. Long scriptural copy loses him — the video carries the story.

personas.name: Lakshmi, 44, Chennai-born in Mumbai
personas.profile: Knows her astrology: sade sati, mahadasha, navgrah. Seeks specific remedial rituals (til-tel abhishek, shani shanti). Feed reader, almost never reels.
personas.behavior: Converts when the ad names the exact remedy and planetary problem she has. Astrological specificity = credibility. Generic 'remove all problems' copy fails; mentioning price feels wrong for graha work.

personas.name: Govind, 62, Kolhapur
personas.profile: Retired clerk devoted to specific temples (Kolhapur Mahalaxmi, Hanuman mandirs, shaktipeeths). Facebook only. Highest repeat rate (25%) but tiny tickets. Immune to persuasion tactics — responds only to WHICH temple and WHICH offering.
personas.behavior: Scans for the temple name he worships. Plain short copy naming temple + offering + date converts; everything else is noise he has learned to skip.
